perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is confidential and non-public, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is merely considered sensitive, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "confidential and non-public" with "merely considered sensitive" significantly weakens the duration of the non-disclosure agreement. Information that is merely sensitive is a lower bar than legally protected confidential information. The term confidential information has a legal definition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MN Stat. § 325C.01)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects "trade secrets," which are defined as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. Reducing the threshold to 'merely considered sensitive' undermines the protection afforded to actual trade secrets under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text weakens the protection of information by changing the standard from "confidential and non-public" to "merely considered sensitive." This contradicts the intent of trade secret laws, as explained by the LLM and implied by the definition of "misappropriation" in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which refers to the acquisition of a "trade secret" by improper means. The original text aligns with the legal concept of trade secrets, while the changed text lowers the bar for protection, potentially undermining the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Custom er agrees that, without the prior written consent of Minnesota Power, it will not m ake any statem ent to any com petitor, cust omer or other third party with respect to the Inform ation exchanged hereunder or regarding the Project itself.
perturbation.changed_text: Custom er agrees that, without the prior written consent of Minnesota Power, it will not m ake any statem ent to any com petitor, cust omer or other third party with respect to the Inform ation exchanged hereunder or regarding the Project itself, unless compelled by legal discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "unless compelled by legal discovery" creates a loophole that could undermine the confidentiality agreement. While legal discovery is a legitimate exception in many contexts, this blanket exception could be exploited to disclose confidential information prematurely or unnecessarily.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26.03
perturbation.law_explanation: While Rule 26.03 of the Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure allows for protective orders to shield confidential information from discovery, this clause does not require the Customer to seek such protection. It creates a possible conflict with the intention of the confidentiality agreement, because it enables uncontrolled release of confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potentially broad exception to the confidentiality agreement, stating information can be disclosed if 'compelled by legal discovery.' The Law Explanation notes that this exception, without requiring the Customer to seek a protective order, could lead to uncontrolled release of confidential information, conflicting with the agreement's intention. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'Minnesota Rules of Court,' implying that the rules of legal discovery are governed elsewhere; therefore, the exception could permit disclosures that the original contract sought to prevent. While legal discovery is a legitimate process, the blanket exception created undermines the original confidentiality agreement, satisfying the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' as contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, Custom er agrees that Minnesota Power shall ha ve the right to obtain a tem porary restraining order enjoining disclosure or use of all Inform ation subject to this Agreem ent. Custom er agrees not to contest the granting of such tem porary restraining order. Such order shall becom e perm anent upon adequate showing of irreparable inju ry and ruling by a court of law. The Parties may participate in any contest relating to a perm anent injunction.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, Custom er agrees that Minnesota Power shall ha ve the right to obtain a tem porary restraining order enjoining disclosure or use of all Inform ation subject to this Agreem ent. Custom er agrees not to contest the granting of such tem porary restraining order. Such order shall becom e perm anent upon showing of any inju ry and ruling by a court of law. The Parties may participate in any contest relating to a perm anent injunction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "irreparable injury" to "any injury" significantly lowers the bar for obtaining a permanent injunction. Irreparable injury is a specific legal standard requiring harm that cannot be adequately compensated by monetary damages. Allowing a permanent injunction for any injury weakens the protections afforded to the Customer and deviates from established legal principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes Section 325C.02 references injunctive relief for misappropriation. Injunctive relief is usually only provided for irreparable harm. By removing the term 'irreparable' this portion of the agreement is contradictory to how courts usually grant injunctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'irreparable injury' for a permanent injunction, aligning with standard legal practice where such injunctions are granted when monetary damages are insufficient. The changed text lowers this standard to 'any injury,' which contradicts the principle that injunctions are typically reserved for situations where the harm is irreparable. This is supported by the Law Explanation, which states that injunctive relief is usually only provided for irreparable harm, and Minnesota Statutes Section 325C.02 references injunctive relief for misappropriation. By removing the term 'irreparable,' the changed agreement deviates from customary court practice.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties. Customer and Minnesota Power acknowledge and agree that no other statements, representations, agreements or warranties, except those outlined herein, apply to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the primary agreement between the Parties. Customer and Minnesota Power acknowledge and agree that no other statements, representations, agreements or warranties, except those outlined herein, apply to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "entire agreement" to "primary agreement" weakens the integration clause. An entire agreement clause is designed to prevent parties from later claiming that there were side agreements or understandings not reflected in the written contract. Using 'primary' opens the door for arguments that other agreements exist and supplement this one.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statute of Frauds, MN Stat. § 513.04
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. An integration clause helps to ensure that the written agreement reflects the parties' complete understanding. Changing 'entire' to 'primary' weakens this protection and could allow parol evidence to alter the terms of the agreement, potentially contradicting the statute of frauds.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 513.04 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes PROPERTY AND PROPERTY INTERESTS Chapter 513 Section 513.04 513.03 513.05 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 513 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 513.04 Version List 513.04 CONVEYANCE OF INTEREST IN LAND EXCEPT UP TO ONE-YEAR LEASE. No estate or interest in lands, other than leases for a term not exceeding one year, nor any trust or power over or concerning lands, or in any manner relating thereto, shall hereafter be created, granted, assigned, surrendered, or declared, unless by act or operation of law, or by deed or conveyance in writing, subscribed by the parties creating, granting, assigning, surrendering, or declaring the same, or by their lawful agent thereunto authorized by writing. This section shall not affect in any manner the power of a testator in the disposition of real estate by will; nor prevent any trust from arising or being extinguished by implication or operation of law. History: ( 8459 ) RL s 3487 ; 1986 c 444 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The change from 'entire agreement' to 'primary agreement' weakens the integration clause, which is designed to ensure the written agreement reflects the parties' complete understanding. The Statute of Frauds, as indicated by Scraped Law Snippet 1 (MN Statute 513.04), requires certain agreements concerning land to be in writing. By weakening the integration clause, the modified text increases the risk that parol evidence could be used to alter the terms of the agreement, potentially contradicting the statute of frauds' requirement for written agreements in land transactions. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the contractual language deviates from the legally recognized need for a complete written record to prevent fraud.
